attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man is being managed in the Intensive Care Unit following major abdominal surgery. He has a medical history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with frequent exacerbations, hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. His regular medications include multiple inhalers, prednisolone, rosuvastatin and amlodipine. He has been taking these as prescribed by his General Practitioner since admission.

On examination, he appears well but reports significant dizziness when sitting upright. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 85/55 mmHg (MAP 65), respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on 2 L/min oxygen, and temperature 36.5°C. His abdomen is soft with mild tenderness around the surgical site, with approximately 200mls of haemoserous fluid in the abdominal drain. He has intravenous fluids running in addition to TPN (total parenteral nutrition).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is likely experiencing adrenal suppression due to his long-term steroid use and the lack of perioperative stress dosing. The hypotension, despite adequate fluid management and with otherwise normal observations, supports this diagnosis. The most appropriate next step is to administer intravenous hydrocortisone to address the adrenal insufficiency.

Whilst giving a 500ml intravenous fluid bolus may transiently improve blood pressure, it does not address the underlying cause of the hypotension.

Starting a vasopressor infusion may be necessary if the patient's hypotension worsens despite steroid administration, but it is not the first step in management. His current mean arterial pressure (MAP) is 65 which is appropriate and vasopressors are therefore not indicated currently, however this is likely still well below the patient's baseline.

There is no indication for a blood transfusion based on the information provided, and the patient's hypotension is less likely due to blood loss. Monitoring drain tube output (eg for large volumes of frank blood output) and haemoglobin levels would be the best way to detect a post-operative bleed.

Performing an abdominal CT scan may be considered if there is suspicion of a surgical complication, but the clinical presentation and examination findings do not currently support this.
learningPoints: Patients on long-term high-dose steroids require stress dosing during periods of significant physiological stress, such as surgery, to prevent adrenal insufficiency.
Adrenal insufficiency can present with hypotension that is resistant to usual measures.
references: Marino LO, Souza HP. Dysfunction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis in critical illness: a narrative review for emergency physicians. Eur J Emerg Med. 2020 Dec;27(6):406-413. doi: 10.1097/MEJ.0000000000000693. PMID: 33108130; PMCID: PMC7587239. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33108130/

Seo KH. Perioperative glucocorticoid management based on current evidence. Anesth Pain Med (Seoul). 2021 Jan;16(1):8-15. doi: 10.17085/apm.20089. Epub 2021 Jan 15. PMID: 33445232; PMCID: PMC7861897. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7861897/
